title: The Toucher
content:
ย ย ย ย He was a jobbing hand from the printers๏ฟฝ flat. His name was Raymond Cato, but he acquired ๏ฟฝToucher๏ฟฝ as a complimentary title when we knew him better. He was tall, sallow, languid and distressingly impecunious. I put it that way because Mr Cato๏ฟฝs impecuniosity was more a trait of character than the result of misfortune. He was the sort of young man who would have been impecunious had he been born to ten thousand a year. He was slovenly in his dress, and his trousers were always worn to strings at the heels, and this fringe collected various foreign bodies, which dragged after him as be walked, Raymond being too languid or too indifferent to shake them off. You got to know when Toucher was coming by the clatter of vagrant articles attached to his trousers fringe. He once towed a disused fish-tin after him through a whole hot afternoon. That will give you an idea of the sort of person Raymond Cato was. But this depraved young man, while apparently sleeping against a case, could paw type with miraculous speed and precision, and he handled the most intricate jobs with absolute certainty when under the influence of two buckets of very bad beer.

ย ย ย ย Mr Cato had only been ten days in the factory when be came to the packer๏ฟฝs board and leaned there. There were two peach-nuts, a metal rule, and the rind off a tin of red ink dangling at his fetlock. He passed his hand wearily over his brow, brushing back his long, black hair, and rested his eyes on the packer. Raymond๏ฟฝs eyes were large and dark, and suffused with an overwhelming sadness. The Toucher owed his success largely to those appealing eyes.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝS๏ฟฝpose we do a break, Mills.๏ฟฝ he said, joylessly.

ย ย ย ย Feathers looked at him with bitter reproach. The remark was an invitation to execute a strategic exit by the lift door, and drink pints, and Feathers was up to his ears in work.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝ๏ฟฝOw th๏ฟฝell can I๏ฟฝ he said, pointing at the long list of orders, ๏ฟฝ๏ฟฝnt๏ฟฝ the whole flamin๏ฟฝ warehouse whoopin๏ฟฝ fer goods?๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝOh, well,๏ฟฝ said Cam, resignedly. ๏ฟฝI had a tizzie, my last and it๏ฟฝs so lonely I reckoned I๏ฟฝd let it go.๏ฟฝ He took the coin out, turned it over in his fingers, and sighed. ๏ฟฝLeft blooming alone,๏ฟฝ he said.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝDown to it, are yeh?๏ฟฝ asked the packer with sympathy. ๏ฟฝThere๏ฟฝve been times when I๏ฟฝve ๏ฟฝad t๏ฟฝ run by ther pub with me eyes shut meself, ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ I know what it is.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝFair on my knuckle-bone,๏ฟฝ said the printer. ๏ฟฝBut a man doesn๏ฟฝt care on his own account. It๏ฟฝs the old lady.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย Feathers looked sidelong. Feathers๏ฟฝs class is always suspicious of sentiment; but there was no snivelling in Raymond๏ฟฝs tone. His expression was that of a strong man who bears his troubles bravely, and his accent hinted at a profound emotion kept well in hand.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝTribulation in thor ๏ฟฝappy ๏ฟฝome?๏ฟฝ queried Feathers, warily.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝSlathers, Mills, old man.๏ฟฝ Raymond Cato turned his shoulder, but the strong composed voice continued presently: ๏ฟฝThe mother, you know. Seen better days, George; good family. Dreadfully ill, and๏ฟฝ, here the voice was almost cold, ๏ฟฝand I haven๏ฟฝt the half-crown to pay for her medicine tonight.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝJimmy Jee!๏ฟฝ murmured Feathers. He fingered a solitary coin in his pocket lovingly, drew it out, and laid it on the bench.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝ๏ฟฝEre๏ฟฝs arf er dollar you can have ther use of,๏ฟฝ he said.

ย ย ย ย There was just the faintest suggestion of a start, the most momentary hint of eagerness, in Raymond๏ฟฝs descent upon the money. The thing had been easier than he expected. Mills noticed the start, and a pang of repentance shot through him. Cato realized his mistake instantly. He placed a firm retainer on the half-crown, and slid it back towards the packer.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝNo, no, old man,๏ฟฝ he said, ๏ฟฝyou can๏ฟฝt spare this.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝGarn!๏ฟฝ retorted the packer, with simulated indifference, ๏ฟฝget a ๏ฟฝammerlock on it.๏ฟฝ With a flash of diplomacy, he added; ๏ฟฝIt๏ฟฝs on๏ฟฝy till Saterdee, anyhow.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝOh, all right, Geordie, till Saturday. You๏ฟฝre a good sort.๏ฟฝ Cato๏ฟฝs tone implied that the time might soon come when he would show his gratitude by dying for the packer. He took up the half-crown slowly, reluctantly, and went gently downstairs.

ย ย ย ย For a minute Feathers gazed fixedly at the blank wall before him, forgetting his work.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝI๏ฟฝve been stabbed,๏ฟฝ he whispered. ๏ฟฝThat๏ฟฝs it, er clean stab. ๏ฟฝN๏ฟฝ I was beginnin๏ฟฝ t๏ฟฝ think I was grown up. Geordie Mills, you ain๏ฟฝt fit t๏ฟฝ be allowed out without yer aunt.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย Feathers was not a man to show his wounds. He said nothing, and in the course of a day or so the town traveller came to him with his trouble.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝWhat of young Cato, the comp with the fatal beauty of a consumptive nun,๏ฟฝ said Goudy; ๏ฟฝis he a confidence trick or what?๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝHe๏ฟฝs ther pride iv ther fam๏ฟฝly, ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ ther sole support iv all his bed-ridden relations,๏ฟฝ replied the packer.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝWell, he๏ฟฝs touched me three times in a week, and I๏ฟฝm as Scotch as most people.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝHow๏ฟฝd he plead? Was it corf drops for his sick sister, ๏ฟฝr fun๏ฟฝral expenses fer his dead brother-in-law left over from last week consequence iv thor ๏ฟฝard ๏ฟฝearted undertaker ref usin๏ฟฝ delivery at ther graveside?๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝI don๏ฟฝt know how he did it,๏ฟฝ mused Goudy, scratching his whiskers. ๏ฟฝHe must have used laughing gas. ๏ฟฝTwas absolutely painless extraction.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝ๏ฟฝE๏ฟฝs er hartist, got er touch like velvet. ๏ฟฝE put๏ฟฝs ther acid on so๏ฟฝt yeh think it๏ฟฝs ther milk iv ๏ฟฝuman kindness.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝHello,๏ฟฝ cried Goudy, ๏ฟฝhe๏ฟฝs dipped up something of yours then, has he?๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย Feathers almost blushed. ๏ฟฝCome off,๏ฟฝ he said, with a shade less than his usual confidence. ๏ฟฝTher gay deceivers don๏ฟฝt twitter t๏ฟฝ me. I๏ฟฝve bin too long out iv th๏ฟฝ egg.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝOh, yes, yes, yes, yes. I was forgetting,๏ฟฝ chortled the town traveller, softly. ๏ฟฝYou are Abdul the Wise and Wonderful. You are the great Take-Down. The bad men go home by the back streets to escape your machinations, and you๏ฟฝve brought hundreds of spielers to destitution.๏ฟฝ And Goudy went about his business, whistling absently the offensive refrain of ๏ฟฝMuggins the Juggins๏ฟฝ.

ย ย ย ย Feathers pasted a label, and attached it to a parcel with unwonted precision. He hated Goudy when he used big words. ๏ฟฝAny๏ฟฝow,๏ฟฝ said Mills, ๏ฟฝI๏ฟฝm not er soft thing for beginners. I๏ฟฝm not sich er snag fer sharpers that ther boys practise on me.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย This little disputation rendered it unnecessary for Feathers to breathe a word about the manner in which the Toucher had stabbed him. The town traveller, too, concluded that his reputation as a business man would not be enhanced by further discussion, and so Raymond Cato was free to play upon the tender buds of human sympathy in the lean and dusty breast of Samuel Ellis, foreman of the flat.

ย ย ย ย Ellis was cutting a ream of tinfoil for which Toucher was waiting, and Toucher๏ฟฝs sadness was dramatic. He sighed heavily, and a tear fell on the polished platform of the guillotine. Raymond gently wiped it off with a piece of waste.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝAren๏ฟฝt you well, then, Mr Cato?๏ฟฝ said Ellis. The woeful foreman even ministered Billy, the personal devil from the printing flat.

ย ย ย ย Raymond Cato started like a guilty thing, moved away a few steps, changed his mind, as if convinced that Fuzzy was the kind of man in whom any stricken soul might confide, returned, and placed the photograph of a baby boy on the platform. The baby was taken on the half-shell, and looked like one of those remarkably fine children who earn an excellent living by posing as examples of the results of feeding on Somebody s infants๏ฟฝ Condition Mixture. The young man had found the photograph in a drawer downstairs that afternoon.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝYou are a father yourself?๏ฟฝ he asked.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝNo๏ฟฝ said Ellis, ๏ฟฝbut I have a sister what is. Five she has.๏ฟฝ And then, with a lugubrious effort to show interest, ๏ฟฝWhat๏ฟฝs he, a boy or a girl?๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝYou wouldn๏ฟฝt know him now,๏ฟฝ Raymond said, huskily. ๏ฟฝSkin and bone, skin and bone. And he cries to me so pitifully; and what can I do?๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝHave you tried castor oil?๏ฟฝ asked Ellis, vaguely.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝMy God!๏ฟฝ said the young man, in a low, terrible voice ๏ฟฝif that boy dies, I don๏ฟฝt care what becomes of me.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝGo on!๏ฟฝ murmured the foreman, deeply touched.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝAnd what๏ฟฝs a man to do?, what can a man do? They order expensive things, chicken in champagne. I tell you, I feel sometimes that I could commit murder to procure the money that might be the saving of my boy.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝI wouldn๏ฟฝt if I was you,๏ฟฝ Ellis said. He was quite agitated. He looked at Cato with pitiful eyes. ๏ฟฝI really wouldn๏ฟฝt you know.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย Raymond snatched up the tinfoil and walked away, stopped, and came back, looked Ellis square in the eye, steadily, for nearly half a minute, and then he said deliberately, emphatically, as if putting the foreman to the test of his life.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝWould you lend me half-a-sovereign?๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝYes, yes; of course,๏ฟฝ stammered Fuzzy. He went to his vest pocket for the money. His hands quite trembled with eagerness as he handed it to Cato.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝBut mind, now, no murders,๏ฟฝ he said.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝDon๏ฟฝt speak to me or I shall break down,๏ฟฝ faltered Raymond Cato, in the character of one unaccustomed to such great kindness and he fled from the flat.

ย ย ย ย Benno๏ฟฝs turn came later. Be also went to Feathers with the tale. He tried to speak as one with unshaken confidence in the Toucher, but there were subtle doubts hovering at the back of his head.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝGot ther luck iv er lame cat, Cato has,๏ฟฝ he said, fiddling with the packer๏ฟฝs scales. ๏ฟฝD๏ฟฝjer hear about him?๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย Feathers fanned out a ream, and knocked it up like a second Cinquevalli; then he sought a hiding place for the incriminating tobacco juice, and spat with judgement.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝBenno,๏ฟฝ he Said, with aggravating conviction, ๏ฟฝyet comin๏ฟฝ t๏ฟฝ me with ther story iv yet shame?๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝGive๏ฟฝs er charnce,๏ฟฝ retorted the clerk.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝTher lad below been nibblin๏ฟฝ yet ear, ๏ฟฝn thinkin๏ฟฝ iv doin๏ฟฝ er mag erbout sheddin๏ฟฝ yer beans in ther sacred cause iv charity, but it don๏ฟฝt go.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝYou๏ฟฝre one what knows,๏ฟฝ sneered Benno.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝWell, er bloke๏ฟฝs lived er bit,๏ฟฝ admitted the oracle. ๏ฟฝChat aloud, Benjamin, I๏ฟฝm waitin๏ฟฝ fer ther ๏ฟฝarrowin๏ฟฝ details. Was it his pore ole father, what was er Dook once, crying all night with dried peas in his appendicitis, him bein๏ฟฝ er victim to ๏ฟฝem ๏ฟฝr his lady mother who๏ฟฝs li๏ฟฝble t๏ฟฝ become sober at any moment if ther charitably inclined don๏ฟฝt come to ๏ฟฝer assistance in ๏ฟฝer hour iv need? ๏ฟฝCause I may tell yeh, Benno ther seraph, that Cato๏ฟฝs parents ๏ฟฝye both bin missin๏ฟฝ for years. They saw what he was comin๏ฟฝ to when he was five, ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ did er guy, leavin๏ฟฝ him t๏ฟฝ ther mercy iv er crool world on er pub doorstep.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝNothin๏ฟฝ like it,๏ฟฝ said the clerek. ๏ฟฝHe had a naxident, swallered er thick-un. I was workin๏ฟฝ a bit late day before yes๏ฟฝday, ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ ๏ฟฝlong erbout art past six who should come sprintin๏ฟฝ upstairs but me nibs, pale๏ฟฝs er blessed egg, hair on end, fair dilly. The bums was in his house fer rent, ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ he was hurryin๏ฟฝ ๏ฟฝome with ther quid he๏ฟฝd got advanced stuck in his tooth box, when he butted into some gazob in ther street, ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ down went ther thick-un. He was tearin๏ฟฝ ratty t๏ฟฝ raise another jim. Er bloke he knows promised him four half dollars, ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ he come t๏ฟฝ me fer ther rest, seein๏ฟฝ I was a cobber in er way, ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ his ๏ฟฝome sweet ๏ฟฝome was goin๏ฟฝ t๏ฟฝ pieces on his ๏ฟฝands. Acourse, I parted me arf jim, couldn๏ฟฝt have ther brick face t๏ฟฝ do less under ther circs. ๏ฟฝTwas on๏ฟฝy fer er day, he said, cause he was goin๏ฟฝ under er operation yes๏ฟฝday ter recover then lost goblin.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝ๏ฟฝN๏ฟฝ was ther operation er triumph iv surgical skill?๏ฟฝ asked Feathers. ๏ฟฝI know, they recovered ten sovs ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ er gold watch, ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ Cato๏ฟฝs payin๏ฟฝ two ๏ฟฝundred per cent dividends t๏ฟฝ ther share holders. Yer goin๏ฟฝ t๏ฟฝ hinvite me out t๏ฟฝ ther parlour bar, ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ plaster me in ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ out with sixpenny drinks.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝYou ain๏ฟฝt bettin๏ฟฝ on that.๏ฟฝ Benno was gloomy now. ๏ฟฝNo; his nibs come t๏ฟฝ me yes๏ฟฝday ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ said he๏ฟฝd seen his medical adviser, ๏ฟฝif the blessed operation ๏ฟฝud cost one ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ a ๏ฟฝarf, ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ that he๏ฟฝd go on with it if I๏ฟฝd contribit another dollar.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝN๏ฟฝ yeh did it, yeh did it!๏ฟฝ yelled Feathers in ecstasy. ๏ฟฝYeh sank another five shillin๏ฟฝ in yer wild cat. Oh, here๏ฟฝs Bertie off ther boat, here๏ฟฝs Little Willie. Some kind gentleman hold his purse while he gets his ๏ฟฝead read. Yeh did it!๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝ๏ฟฝTain๏ฟฝt possible,๏ฟฝ retorted Benno.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝOh, ain๏ฟฝt it? Well, y๏ฟฝ orter wear ear muffs this bitin๏ฟฝ weather, silly boy.๏ฟฝ Feathers slapped at Benno with feminine affectation. ๏ฟฝWhy, Cato never had no ๏ฟฝome that it ๏ฟฝud pay to put er bailiff in at tuppence er day. He๏ฟฝs got er leaky room at mother Spargo๏ฟฝs, in Williamson Street, fer which ๏ฟฝe owes regular every Fridee, ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ he dosses on er two-feather bed, weather permittin๏ฟฝ.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย Evidently it was Mr Cato๏ฟฝs intention to try the acid on Feathers again. He approached the packer a few days after Benno๏ฟฝs confession, and he had the air of one patient under great provocation. The packer๏ฟฝs hand went to his ear instinctively.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝHow would you take a good thing for the Handicap tomorrow. Feathers?๏ฟฝ said the comp. ๏ฟฝYou๏ฟฝre the straightest man here, and the only one I๏ฟฝd let into this with me.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย The packer did not reply for a few moments. He lingered for effect, chewing absently.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝThis ther one yeh worked on Billy?๏ฟฝ He spoke without interest.

ย ย ย ย Raymond looked at Mills with the reproachful eyes of a starved dog.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝYou don๏ฟฝt trust me, George,๏ฟฝ he said.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝChattin๏ฟฝ iv good things,๏ฟฝ continued Feathers, ๏ฟฝwhat๏ฟฝs gone wrong with that beautiful arf-dollar iv mine?๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝDid you lend me half-a-dollar? Of course, of course, you did, Oh, that๏ฟฝs all right, old fellow.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝThat๏ฟฝs er weight off me mind,๏ฟฝ sighed Feathers. ๏ฟฝI๏ฟฝve bin expectin๏ฟฝ that two ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ er tanner home. I๏ฟฝve bin sittin๏ฟฝ up iv nights waitin๏ฟฝ fer it comin๏ฟฝ, ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ I got t๏ฟฝ thinkin๏ฟฝ yeh was neglectin๏ฟฝ me. Ray, ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ devotin๏ฟฝ all yer money t๏ฟฝ good works.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย The comp๏ฟฝs sadness was intensified by Mills๏ฟฝ sarcasm. ๏ฟฝSurely a miserable half dollar wouldn๏ฟฝt hurt you,๏ฟฝ he said.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝIt ๏ฟฝurts me pride,๏ฟฝ answered Feathers. ๏ฟฝIt ๏ฟฝurts me t๏ฟฝ think I๏ฟฝve bin done on ther grid. The hagony๏ฟฝs somethin๏ฟฝ awful, ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ I๏ฟฝve gotter get that ๏ฟฝarf-bull ๏ฟฝr sometin๏ฟฝ dangerous may set in.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝOh, very well, you shall have it immediately.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝDon๏ฟฝt get runnin๏ฟฝ me down with it, Ray, gi๏ฟฝ me time t๏ฟฝ break ther glad tidin๏ฟฝs t๏ฟฝ me fam๏ฟฝly.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย Raymond Cato spent the dinner-hour discussing the drama and kindred arts with Martha Pilcher, and in the afternoon he sent the half-crown to Mills in an envelope inscribed, ๏ฟฝWith R.C.๏ฟฝs compliments.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย By this time the Toucher had almost exhausted the male employees. There were many aching victims downstairs, and his title was recognized all through the factory. He paid a pressing creditor when he could borrow the necessary sum from another subject, but that did not happen often. Strangers were continually asking for him at the door in the lane, and people would await his coming for an hour after he had gone home over the adjoining roofs, and down via an accommodating tea warehouse with an open-air staircase. Raymond borrowed only small sums from the Beauties, but he was very successful with them. The other young men always approached the girls in a mood of artful and distressing levity, but the Toucher was very grave with them, and always sympathetic whatever the complaint might be, and there was scarcely a Beauty who hadn๏ฟฝt a complaint of some kind by her, if only as a convenient theme of conversation. Half-a-dozen of them would have died for the bibulous comp despite the bottle-oh๏ฟฝs stock garnered in the trouser fringe at his boot heels, and every paster but one seemed good for a shilling in exchange for a sigh or two and a pathetic glance from his soulful eyes. Those eyes were irresistible when Raymond was really thirsty.

ย ย ย ย The one girl whom Raymond had not been seen to approach for a little loan was Miss Eva Magill. Miss Eva was not as young as some, and she had no ๏ฟฝcobbers๏ฟฝ amongst the Beauties. She was demure and steadfast, came softly into the factory with down cast eyes, worked quietly and persistently in an out-of-the-way corner, with drooping lids, and passed out again, noiselessly, with eyes unlifted, and was wholly unknown. She was disliked and respected by the whole factory.

ย ย ย ย Toucher๏ฟฝs finish was sudden and dramatic. One afternoon faint echoes of a warm debate came up from the warehouse. Presently Miss Magill was sent for. She went down, with her apron over her face, weeping. She returned in a quarter of an hour, still weeping, went to the dressing-room corner, and had a fit of hysterics, concluding with a dead faint.

ย ย ย ย Billy the Boy bobbed up a few moments later. ๏ฟฝEr fair ole beano!๏ฟฝ he gasped. ๏ฟฝThey ye dumped Toucher in ther dust-box for immedjit removal. Spats wanted t๏ฟฝ send fer the Johns, He๏ฟฝs done Magill in fer ๏ฟฝer little bit.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย A voice of authority roared below, and Billy ducked down again, but Feathers had the whole story before evening.

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝ๏ฟฝE bin givin๏ฟฝ Magill guff erbout marriage,๏ฟฝ Mills explained to the town traveller, ๏ฟฝ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ ther spiel was good fer fifteen jim ther savin๏ฟฝs iv er lifetime, which ther dear boy was goin๏ฟฝ t risk in ther comfits of a ๏ฟฝome, but which ๏ฟฝe๏ฟฝs bounced down et ther two-up.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝThat pale maiden lady with the little lisp?๏ฟฝ exclaimed Goudy, in amazement. ๏ฟฝWhy, the very thoughts of man gave her all the unfavourable symptoms mentioned in a quack advertisement.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝG๏ฟฝout, that sort๏ฟฝs sweet ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ easy t๏ฟฝ ther lad that ain๏ฟฝt punched off with a frown,๏ฟฝ retorted Feathers. ๏ฟฝHer brother took er tumble, ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ he๏ฟฝs bin here, wantin๏ฟฝ t๏ฟฝ hit Cato clean out, ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ put him away over ther ๏ฟฝolidays for false pretensions, ๏ฟฝr larceny ez er bailiff, ๏ฟฝr somethin๏ฟฝ, only th๏ฟฝ girl wouldn๏ฟฝt hear iv it. She๏ฟฝs bin squealin๏ฟฝ roun๏ฟฝ all ther afternoon that Raymond๏ฟฝs true to ๏ฟฝer, true ez true, she don๏ฟฝt care.๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝThe infernal scoundrel,๏ฟฝ snorted Goudy. ๏ฟฝHe ought to be hanged!๏ฟฝ

ย ย ย ย ๏ฟฝ๏ฟฝE๏ฟฝs er naughty tease,๏ฟฝ Mills soliloquized, ๏ฟฝ๏ฟฝn๏ฟฝ ๏ฟฝe did Scotty fer er tray-bit, ๏ฟฝangin๏ฟฝs too good fer him!๏ฟฝ
meaning:
Edward Dyson's contribution to classic is further solidified by the brilliance found in "The Toucher"...
